titleStmt.title: Lyrical Ballads, 1800: Inscription / Lines Written in Pencil on Stone

analytic.title: Inscription / Lines Written in Pencil on Stone

imprint.publisher: T. N. Longman and O. Rees

revisionDesc.change: Splurged out main xml into separate xml files for poems and paratexts.

head.add:
Lines written with a pencil upon a
Stone in the wall of the House (an Out-
-house on the Island at Grasmere.

del.emph:
INSCRIPTION
For the House (an Outhouse) on the Island at Grasmere.

lg.l: Rude is this Edifice, and Thou hast seen
Buildings, albeit rude, that have maintain’d
Proportions more harmonious, and approach’d
To somewhat of a closer fellowship
With the ideal grace. Yet as it is
Do take it in good part; for he, the poor
Vitruvius of our village, had no help
/C From the great city; never on the leaves
Of red Morocco folio saw display’d
The skeletons and pre-existing ghosts
Of Beauties yet unborn, the rustic Box,
Snug Cot, with Coach-house, Shed and Hermitage.
/P It is a homely pile, yet to these walls
The heifer comes in the snow-storm, and here
The new-dropp’d lamb finds shelter from the wind.
And hither does one Poet sometimes row
/P His pinnace, a small vagrant barge, up-piled B/
With plenteous store of heath and wither’d fern,
( ^ A lading which he with his sickle cuts
Among the mountains, ) ^ and beneath this roof
He makes his summer couch, and here at noon
Spreads out his limbs, while, yet unborn ^ unshorn, the sheep S/
Panting beneath the burthen of their wool
Lie round him, even as if they were a part
/H Of his own household: nor, while from his bed
He through that door-place looks toward the lake
And to the stirring breezes, does he want
Creations lovely as the work of sleep,
Fair sights, and visions of romantic joy.
